target.name: daphlongeranine B
target.motivation: The daphlongeranine subfamily is a unique structural outlier within the Daphniphyllum alkaloids, characterized by an embedded 1-azaspiro[4.4]nonane bicyclic motif and three quaternary centers. There are no prior literature reports on their synthesis, making an approach to their core a worthwhile objective for advancing the field and impacting synthetic approaches toward other congested sp3-rich architectures.

success_criteria.free_text: Synthesis of the tetracyclic core of the daphlongeranines in 11 steps, with proof-of-concept for an enantioselective synthesis.

key_disconnections.reasoning: This bond formation is crucial for constructing the tricyclic core. Initial attempts with reductive Heck conditions led to undesired cyclopropanation, necessitating a revision to achieve selective conjugate addition.

alternatives_considered.rejected_because: Generally little to no observation of desired reactivity.
Generally little to no observation of desired reactivity.
Generally little to no observation of desired reactivity.
Generally little to no observation of desired reactivity.

key_disconnections.reasoning: This cyclization forms the final ring of the tetracyclic core. It is preceded by a Giese addition to install the three-carbon unit.
This three-step sequence efficiently forms the 1-azaspiro[4.4]nonane bicyclic motif from a proline-derived starting material.

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: The retrosynthetic analysis involved masking the tertiary amine as a C3 amide and protecting the C24 alcohol. The lactone was retraced to a C7 ketone. The pyrrolidinone A ring was simplified to a pyrrolidine, with functionality to be installed later. The complex tetracycle 3 was identified as a key strategic target. Retrosynthetic excision of a three-carbon unit from the seven-membered D ring returned a simplified tricyclic core. A 1,1-disubstituted alkene motif at C4 was found to be most amenable to C-C bond formation from the corresponding functionalized 1-azaspiro[4.4]nonenone bicyclic core, which was proposed to arise from a three-step reductive spirocyclization cascade from a proline-derived starting material.

thought.background: L-proline benzyl ester hydrochloride is a readily available starting material.
thought.gap: Need to oxidize the proline derivative to the corresponding 1,2-nitrone.
thought.decision: To form the 1,2-nitrone, chose a metal-free Oxone-mediated oxidation for better reproducibility, expecting a similar yield to previously reported metal-catalyzed methods.

outcome.observation: Oxone-mediated oxidation afforded the nitrone in good yield with greater reproducibility compared to metal-catalyzed methods.

evidence.text_span: For the forward synthesis, L-proline benzyl ester hydrochloride was free-based and oxidized to the corresponding 1,2- nitrone (Scheme 2). In our initial report detailing the synthesis of spirocyclic pyrrolidines, Na2WO4.- or MeReO3-catalyzed oxidations were employed;42,43 however, these were substituted for a metal-free Oxone-mediated oxidation which afforded a similar yield but with greater reproducibility.44

thought.gap: Need to perform a 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition with an alkyne to build part of the spirocyclic core.
thought.decision: To form the isoxazoline, chose a thermal 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition with alkyne 8, expecting high yield.

outcome.observation: Subsequent thermal 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition with alkyne 8 occurred in >80% yield.

evidence.text_span: Subsequent thermal 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition with alkyne 8 occurred in >80% yield.

thought.gap: Need to exchange the benzyl ester for a ketophosphonate moiety.
thought.decision: To introduce the ketophosphonate, chose addition of the corresponding Li-phosphonate, expecting high efficiency.

outcome.observation: Exchange of the benzyl ester for the desired ketophosphonate moiety, by addition of the corresponding Li-phosphonate, was very efficient.

evidence.text_span: Exchange of the benzyl ester for the desired ketophosphonate moiety, by addition of the corresponding Li-phosphonate, was very efficient, setting the stage for the key spirocyclization reaction.

thought.gap: Need to perform the key spirocyclization reaction to form the N-Boc-amine 11.
thought.decision: To effect spirocyclization, chose treatment with n-BuLi to protect the phosphonate, followed by sodium naphthalenide (NaNap) for N-O reductive cleavage, and in situ Boc-protection, expecting good yield.

outcome.observation: The desired spirocyclic N-Boc-amine 11 was afforded in good yield after basic aqueous work up and in situ Boc-protection.

evidence.text_span: Following treatment with n-BuLi to protect the phosphonate moiety from reduction, then sodium naphthalenide (NaNap) to effect N−O reductive cleavage, the desired spirocyclic N-Boc-amine 11 could be afforded in good yield, after basic aqueous work up and in situ Boc-protection.41

thought.background: Spirocyclic N-Boc-amine 11 is available. The next step is to install a functional handle for C4-C5 cyclization.
thought.gap: Need to find a suitable cyclization mode to form the C4-C5 bond.
thought.decision: Explored various cyclization modes including Michael reactivity, radical addition, MHAT chemistry, and cyclopropanation via diazo precursors, expecting to find a viable route.

reaction.named_reaction: Michael reactivity, radical addition, MHAT chemistry, cyclopropanation via diazo precursors

outcome.observation: After extensive investigation, generally little to no observation of desired reactivity was found for these cyclization modes.

evidence.text_span: At this point, attention turned to the installation of a functional handle amenable for the C4−C5 cyclization (Scheme 3). Amide substrates could be generated trivially via Boc-deprotection and amide coupling. Hence, a broad survey of potential cyclization modes was conducted, including but not limited to Michael reactivity, radical addition,45 MHAT chemistry, and cyclopropanation via diazo precursors.33,46−48 After extensive investigation, with generally little to no observation of desired reactivity, successful C−C bond formation was observed with the treatment of α-bromoacrylamide 12 under reductive Heck conditions

thought.background: Previous cyclization attempts were unsuccessful. Reductive Heck conditions are being explored.
thought.gap: Need to achieve C-C bond formation for the C4-C5 cyclization.
thought.decision: To achieve C-C bond formation, chose treatment of α-bromoacrylamide 12 under reductive Heck conditions (Jeffrey’s conditions), expecting cyclization.

outcome.observation: Successful C-C bond formation was observed, but the reaction unexpectedly led to an additional cyclopropane ring, forming Mecyclopropane 13, rather than the desired single carbopalladation product. This demonstrated proof-of-concept for Pd-mediated cyclization but with undesired selectivity.

evidence.text_span: After extensive investigation, with generally little to no observation of desired reactivity, successful C−C bond formation was observed with the treatment of α-bromoacrylamide 12 under reductive Heck conditions (Pd(OAc)2, HCO2Na, Et3N, TBACl, DMF, 80 °C, 18 h − Jeffrey’s conditions, Scheme 3A).49 Remarkably, rather than undergoing solely a single carbopalladation event and subsequent reductive termination, the resulting palladium enolate underwent a further intramolecular carbopalladation, and reductive termination occurred from the alkyl-Pd species to afford Mecyclopropane 13. While this additional cyclopropane ring-forming reactivity was undesired, the reaction demonstrated proof-of-concept for the application of Pd-mediated cyclization in forging the C4−C5 bond.50

thought.background: The reductive Heck reaction led to undesired cyclopropanation. The goal is to obtain a more useful intermediate by diverting the reaction course.
thought.gap: Need to modify the substrate to achieve selective C4-C5 bond formation without cyclopropanation.
thought.decision: Investigated installing a cyclopentane/ene at the α-position of the cyclopentenone (C6) via iodination and Suzuki-Miyaura coupling, followed by hydrogenation, and then forming the corresponding α-bromoacrylamides (18, 19). Then, explored Heck-type cyclization conditions, expecting to divert the reaction.

outcome.observation: Under either Heck or reductive Heck conditions, no or only trace cyclization was observed for the modified α-bromoacrylamides (18, 19).

evidence.text_span: Variation of either the α position of the cyclopentenone (C6) or the β position of the unsaturated amide (C17) was proposed to divert the course of the reaction to afford more useful intermediates than cyclopropane 13. Initially, the α- installation of a cyclopentane/ene was investigated, reflecting the daphlongeranine E ring. Iodination and subsequent Suzuki-Miyaura coupling with cyclopenteneboronic acid pinacol ester was facile, affording 15. 51 Rh/C-catalyzed hydrogenation was effective for accessing the cyclopentane analogue 16. Following synthesis of the corresponding α-bromoacrylamides (18, 19), investigation of the Heck-type cyclization confirmed that, under either Heck or reductive Heck conditions, no or only trace cyclization was observed.52,53

thought.background: Modifying the α-position with a cyclopentane/ene did not lead to successful cyclization. Another approach to divert the Heck reaction is needed.
thought.gap: Need to find a way to achieve the targeted diene product with better yield.
thought.decision: To achieve the diene product, chose Suzuki-Miyaura coupling to install a methyl group at C6, offering a potential β-hydrogen elimination pathway, and then subjecting the corresponding α-bromoacrylamide 20 to select Heck conditions, expecting improved yield.

outcome.observation: While the targeted diene product was afforded, the yield was low (21%) and could not be raised.

evidence.text_span: Alternatively, Suzuki-Miyaura coupling could install a methyl group at C6,54 offering a potential β-hydrogen elimination pathway following the first carbopalladation in the Heck reaction. While, under select Heck conditions,49 the corresponding α-bromoacrylamide 20 did afford the targeted diene product, the yield was low and could not be raised above 21%.

thought.background: Modifying the α-position did not yield satisfactory results. The β-position of the unsaturated amide is another point of variation.
thought.gap: Need to find a way to achieve a useful cyclization product with better yield.
thought.decision: To achieve a useful cyclization product, chose to vary the β-position of the unsaturated amide by using crotonamide 21 in the Heck reaction, expecting a different cyclization pathway.

outcome.observation: Vinylcyclopropene 24 was observed, but only in a low yield that made application to the total synthesis unfeasible.

evidence.text_span: Equally, varying the β position of the unsaturated amide, crotonamide 21 was trialled in the Heck reaction; pleasingly, vinylcyclopropene 24 was observed, however only in a low yield that made application to the total synthesis unfeasible.52,53

thought.background: The original reductive Heck cyclopropanation reaction (step 6) produced an undesired cyclopropane. Subsequent modifications to the substrate (steps 7-9) were unsuccessful in diverting the reaction to a useful product.
thought.gap: Need to modify the reaction conditions of the original reductive Heck cyclopropanation to achieve a single Pd-mediated conjugate addition, forming the desired tricyclic product 4.
thought.decision: To obtain the desired tricyclic product 4, chose to revisit the original reductive Heck cyclopropanation reaction and carefully modify the reaction conditions, specifically by varying the H-source and halide salt, expecting to deviate the pathway from double carbopalladation to a single Pd-mediated conjugate addition.

outcome.observation: Initial variations of the H-source (i-PrOH or AcOH) gave low yields of 4. Switching TBACl to TBABr and increasing equivalents improved yield to 37%. The combination of a tertiary amine, strong acid, TBAI, and PPh3 as a ligand led to a substantial increase in the yield of 4 to 70% NMR yield. This optimized condition successfully achieved the desired tricyclic core 4 via selective Pd-mediated conjugate addition.

evidence.text_span: Given these challenges, the original reductive Heck cyclopropanation reaction was revisited in the hope that, by careful modification of the reaction conditions, the pathway could be deviated from a double carbopalladation to a single Pdmediated conjugate addition (Scheme 4A, entry 1; see also Supporting Information S1). Initially, by varying the H-source from HCO2Na to either i-PrOH or AcOH, a low yield of the desired tricyclic product 4 was observed (entries 2 and 3). In cases where a carboxylic acid was employed as the H-source, a non-negligible degree of γ-coupled product 26 was observed, likely from the corresponding (extended) enol coupling mode (entry 3). The yield of 4 could be further increased to 37% by switching TBACl to TBABr and increasing the equivalents from one to three (entry 5). Investigating alternative reductive Heck conditions led predominantly to direct reduction, i.e., proto-depalladation of the initial oxidative addition intermediate (entry 4). In 1989, Cacchi reported that the combination of a tertiary amine and strong acid such as trifluoroacetic acid, in the presence of tert-butylammonium iodide (TBAI) as a phase transfer catalyst, led to preferential formation of conjugate addition products in intermolecular Heck reactions, over traditional Heck-type vinyl substitution products.55 Trialling these conditions gave a comparable yield to the previous optimal conditions but notably with considerable unreacted starting material observed (32%, entry 7). Increasing the quantity of TBAI and inclusion of PPh3 as a ligand led to a substantial increase in the yield of 4 to 70% NMR yield (entries 8, 9, and 10). It should be noted that XAT-mediated radical cyclization and organocuprate formation were also attempted, and the Pd-mediated conditions proved singularly effective (See Supporting Information S2).

thought.background: Robust access to the tricyclic core 4 is established.
thought.gap: Need to install the seven-membered ring by adding a three-carbon unit.
thought.decision: To install the three-carbon unit, chose XAT-mediated radical addition of 1-bromopropan-3-ol, expecting moderate yield and good diastereoselectivity, while minimizing protecting group manipulations.

outcome.observation: Photocatalytic silyl radical-mediated conditions proved optimal, installing the desired three-carbon unit in moderate yield (38% isolated, 60% crude NMR) and good diastereoselectivity (5.1 d.r.). Initial Co-catalyzed conditions gave low yield (16%).

evidence.text_span: With robust access to a tricyclic core containing differentiated functionality at C4 and C6, the installation of the seven-membered ring could then be investigated (Scheme 4B). In order to minimize nonstrategic protecting group manipulations, the unprotected three-carbon unit 1-bromopropan-3-ol was investigated under XAT-mediated radical addition. Initially, Co-catalyzed conditions reported by Escobar and Johannes afforded the desired product in a low yield (16%, 60% recovered SM);56 however, photocatalytic silyl radicalmediated conditions proved optimal,57 installing the desired three-carbon unit in moderate yield and good diastereoselectivity (60% crude NMR yield, 5.1 d.r., 38% isolated).

thought.background: The three-carbon unit is installed, forming compound 27.
thought.gap: Need to oxidize the alcohol to an aldehyde to set up the intramolecular aldol reaction.
thought.decision: To oxidize the alcohol, chose Dess-Martin Periodinane (DMP), expecting trivial conversion.

outcome.observation: Oxidation to the aldehyde (28) could be conducted trivially with Dess-Martin Periodinane (DMP).

evidence.text_span: Oxidation to the aldehyde could be conducted trivially with Dess-Martin Periodinane (DMP), setting the stage for the intramolecular aldol reaction to close the seven-membered ring.

thought.background: Aldehyde 28 is formed, ready for intramolecular aldol reaction.
thought.gap: Need to close the seven-membered ring via intramolecular aldol reaction.
thought.decision: To close the ring, chose treatment with p-TsOH, expecting cyclization.

outcome.observation: Treatment with p-TsOH led to observation of an undesired dimeric product.

evidence.text_span: Treatment with p-TsOH led to observation of an undesired dimeric product.

thought.background: Treatment with p-TsOH for aldol cyclization led to an undesired dimeric product.
thought.gap: Need to find conditions that successfully cyclize aldehyde 28 to the desired enone 29.
thought.decision: To achieve cyclization, chose treatment with methanolic hydroxide and heating to form the aldol product, followed by heating the mixture with p-TsOH in C6D6 to convert the aldol product to the desired enone 29, expecting smooth conversion.

outcome.observation: Treatment with methanolic hydroxide and heating led to cyclization to a mixture of the aldol product and the corresponding elimination product. Heating this mixture with p-TsOH in C6D6 led to smooth conversion of the aldol product to the desired enone 29 in 35% yield over three steps (from 27).

evidence.text_span: Alternatively, treatment with methanolic hydroxide and heating lead to cyclization to a mixture of the aldol product and the corresponding elimination product. Heating of this mixture with p-TsOH in C6D6 led to smooth conversion of the aldol product to the desired enone 29 in 35% yield over three steps.

thought.background: The tetracyclic core has been synthesized. Now, an enantioselective synthesis of spirocycle 11 is desired to demonstrate the applicability of the strategy for natural product synthesis.
thought.gap: Need to achieve high enantioselectivity and diastereoselectivity in the nitrone/alkyne 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition.
thought.decision: To achieve enantioselective synthesis, chose to investigate enantioselective and diastereoselective variants of the nitrone/alkyne 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition, expecting high selectivity.

outcome.observation: Enantioselective and diastereoselective variants of the nitrone/alkyne 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition were investigated but were unsuccessful in affording the corresponding isoxazoline with high enantioselectivity or diastereoselectivity.

evidence.text_span: Enantioselective and diastereoselective variants of the nitrone/alkyne 1,3- dipolar cycloaddition were investigated but were unsuccessful in affording the corresponding isoxazoline with high enantioselectivity or diastereoselectivity.

thought.background: The nitrone/alkyne cycloaddition failed to provide high enantioselectivity.
thought.gap: Need a new strategy for enantioselective synthesis of spirocycle 11.
thought.decision: To achieve enantioselective synthesis, chose a strategy inspired by Kałuża's work involving alkylation and Parham-type cyclization of Seebach adducts, expecting to access spiro-indane-2,2'-pyrrolidine containing ligands.

outcome.observation: Alkylation of the Seebach adduct occurred in moderate yield (40%) but good diastereoselectivity.

evidence.text_span: Accordingly, a new strategy was developed, inspired by work from Kałuża in which alkylation and Parham-type cyclization of Seebach adducts was employed to access spiro-indane-2,2′-pyrrolidine containing ligands.58,59 By modification of literature procedures, fourcarbon allylic electrophile 31 was accessed in two steps. Alkylation of the Seebach adduct occurred in moderate yield but good diastereoselectivity.

thought.background: The alkylated Seebach adduct 32 is formed.
thought.gap: Need to perform the Parham-type cyclization to form spirocycle 33.
thought.decision: To form spirocycle 33, chose treatment with t-BuLi to effect rapid lithium-iodine exchange and concomitant cyclization, followed by Boc2O, expecting good yield.

outcome.observation: Treatment with t-BuLi effected rapid lithium-iodine exchange and concomitant cyclization to spirocycle 33 in good yield (81%).

evidence.text_span: Treatment with t-BuLi effected rapid lithium−iodine exchange and concomitant cyclization to spirocycle 33 in good yield.

thought.gap: Need to complete the synthesis of enantioenriched spirocycle 11.
thought.decision: To complete the synthesis, chose a three-step γ-Rubottom protection sequence, expecting efficient conversion.

outcome.observation: A three-step γ-Rubottom protection sequence efficiently completed the synthesis of enantioenriched spirocycle 11 in 34% yield over three steps.

evidence.text_span: A three-step γ-Rubottom protection sequence efficiently completed the synthesis of enantioenriched spirocycle 11.

Overall Yield.interpretation: Overall yield for the 11-step synthesis of the tetracyclic core is not explicitly stated, but individual step yields are provided.

Longest Linear Steps.interpretation: The synthesis of the tetracyclic core was achieved in 11 steps.

Total Steps.interpretation: Total number of reported experimental steps, including failed attempts and enantioselective proof-of-concept.

Failed Attempts Count.interpretation: Four distinct failed attempts were reported before successful conditions were found or revised.

validation.significance: The first synthesis of the tetracyclic core of the daphlongeranine natural product family, featuring a unique tricyclic core and two quaternary carbons. The route involved a three-step spirocyclization strategy and the development of a new intramolecular Pd-catalyzed cyclization reaction. An enantioselective synthesis of the bicyclic core was also demonstrated, paving the way for total synthesis of the natural products.

source.title: Synthesis of the Tetracyclic Core of the Daphlongeranines.
source.authors: Shennan BDA, Smith PW, Ogura Y, Källström EA, Moustakim M, Balan T, Dixon DJ.
